reasoning:
The resolution requires cloture to be invoked on a debatable matter with less than a three-fifths majority, which specifically entails changing Rule XXII or setting a new precedent via the 'nuclear option.' While Senate Republicans are currently planning to bypass the filibuster to pass tax cuts, they are doing so using the budget reconciliation process. Even if they overrule the parliamentarian on the Byrd Rule, this occurs within the fast-track reconciliation procedure and does not involve changing or invoking cloture under Rule XXII, thus not satisfying the resolution criteria. 

Looking ahead over the 4-year horizon (119th and 120th Congresses), the risk of eliminating/weakening the legislative filibuster is real but constrained. Republicans currently hold 53 seats, meaning a rule change via the nuclear option requires 50 votes (plus VP Vance). There is a solid core of Republican institutionalists who have vocally opposed eliminating the filibuster, including Majority Leader John Thune, Mitch McConnell, Thom Tillis (who said he would resign if it happened), Susan Collins, and Lisa Murkowski. These five alone would be enough to defeat a nuclear option maneuver (48-52). 

However, the probability is non-negligible because: 1) Trump may exert extreme pressure on the GOP to pass non-budgetary priorities (border wall, etc.) if blocked by Democrats; 2) The 2026 midterms could shift the composition of the GOP Senate caucus toward more MAGA-aligned members, potentially replacing some institutionalists in the 120th Congress; and 3) A 'mini-nuke' (e.g., eliminating the filibuster only for the motion to proceed) would also trigger a YES. Due to the 4-year timeline and increasing partisan polarization, the chance is around 26%.
